coredata.dc:title: An ideal ratiometric fluorescent probe provided by the surface modification of carbon dots for the determination of Pb2+
coredata.dc:creator: Paydar, Sardar
Feizi, Foroozan
Shamsipur, Mojtaba
Barati, Ali
Chehri, Neda
Taherpour, Avat (Arman)
Jamshidi, Morteza
coredata.dc:description: Herein, carbon dots (Cdots) with a single green emission peak and no response to metal ions were converted to a high sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of Pb2+ through a simple chemical surface modification with glutathione. The as-prepared modified Cdots show two emission peaks at a 170 nm distance (from 522 to 692 nm) that were excited with a single excitation wavelength (390 nm) and responded oppositely to Pb2+. This probe provided an ideal ratiometric fluorescent probe for the determination of Pb2+ in a linear concentration range of 10–700 nM with a detection limit of 2.7 nM. Theoretical calculations based on density functional theory (DFT) theory demonstrated that the surface modification of Cdots with glutathione forms new emitting centers on the surface of Cdots that were suppressed through a photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process from the sulfur lone pair electrons of the surface glutathione ligands. However, in the presence of Pb2+, chemical binding between the sulfide groups of surface glutathione ligands of neighboring Cdots and Pb2+ aggregated Cdots. Upon this complex formation, the involved PET process was stopped and subsequently a new emission peak at longer wavelengths appeared.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Lead
Fluorescent probe
Ratiometric
DFT

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of m-AP-GSH Cdots to the determination of Pb2+
Determination of Pb2+ in real water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the support of Iran Nanotechnology Initiative Council (INIC).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM image of the m-AP-GSH Cdots, b) FTIR spectra and c) absorption spectra of the GSH, m-AP Cdots, m-AP-GSH Cdots, and m-AP-GSH Cdots + Pb2+, and d) emission spectra of the m-AP-GSH Cdots at different excitation wavelength (the inset shows emission color of m-AP-GSH Cdots under room and UV (365 nm) light irradiation.

a) Variation of the fluorescence emission intensity of a 15 µg mL−1 of m-AP-GSH Cdots with the gradual addition of Pb2+ ion from 10 to 1000 nM. b) The linear ratiometric fluorescence emission (F692/F522) of the m-AP-GSH Cdots vs Pb2+ concentration over the range of 0–1000 nM. Inset shows the visual fluorescence emissions of 30 µg mL−1 of m-AP-GSH Cdots solution in the the presence of Pb2+ ions under daylight and UV irradiation (λex = 365 nm). c) Fluorescence spectra of 15 µg mL−1 of m-AP-GSH Cdots in the presence of 1000 nM of different metal ions.

Hydrodynamic diameters of a) m-AP-GSH Cdots and b) m-AP-GSH Cdots in the presence of Pb2+ ions measured by DLS. The inset of these two figures shows the corresponding Zeta potential and TEM images. c) fluorescence spectra and d) Rayleigh scattering spectra of m-AP-GSH Cdots after addition of Pb2+ ions (800 nM) and EDTA (800 nM).
The molecular structure, absorption, and emission spectra of the modeled structure form-AP-GSH Cdots (a), and for its 2:1 complex with Pb2+ ion (b).

Different fragments of the modeled structure of a) m-AP-GSH Cdots and b) its complexed form with Pb2+ in terms of electro-optical properties along with the heat map hole-electron density distribution calculations for the assigned excitation states. Red is the most intense and dark blue is the least values.
Holes and electrons orbitals for the modeled structure of m-AP-GSH Cdots in six assigned excitation wavelengths, and participating HOMO and LUMO orbitals.
Schematic illustration of the two-step synthesis of m-AP-GSH Cdots.

head.ce:title: An ideal ratiometric fluorescent probe provided by the surface modification of carbon dots for the determination of Pb2+

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Methodology, Study design, Investigation, Writing – original draft
Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Project administration
Methodology, Study design, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, carbon dots (Cdots) with a single green emission peak and no response to metal ions were converted to a high sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of Pb2+ through a simple chemical surface modification with glutathione. The as-prepared modified Cdots show two emission peaks at a 170 nm distance (from 522 to 692 nm) that were excited with a single excitation wavelength (390 nm) and responded oppositely to Pb2+. This probe provided an ideal ratiometric fluorescent probe for the determination of Pb2+ in a linear concentration range of 10–700 nM with a detection limit of 2.7 nM. Theoretical calculations based on density functional theory (DFT) theory demonstrated that the surface modification of Cdots with glutathione forms new emitting centers on the surface of Cdots that were suppressed through a photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process from the sulfur lone pair electrons of the surface glutathione ligands. However, in the presence of Pb2+, chemical binding between the sulfide groups of surface glutathione ligands of neighboring Cdots and Pb2+ aggregated Cdots. Upon this complex formation, the involved PET process was stopped and subsequently a new emission peak at longer wavelengths appeared.

ce:section.ce:para: Pb2+ is one of the most toxic heavy metal ions that can cause irreparable damages to environment and human health even at the trace concentration levels [1]. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has set the maximum allowable concentration of this ion in public drinking waters at 15 µg L−1, i.e. about 72 nM [2]. Such a low tolerable concentration level indicates the necessity of sensitive and reliable sensing methods to detect low levels of this toxic metal ion. Analytical techniques such as atomic absorption spectrometry [3], inductively coupled plasma [4], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy [5], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [6] are the conventional techniques usually applied to determine Pb2+ concentration. However, these techniques often need complex and expensive equipment, and their determination process is time-consuming [3,4]. Therefore, it is highly important to come up with novel, simple, inexpensive, and precise methods for quick and in situ detection of toxic heavy metal ions such as Pb2+.

Due to the sensitivity, reliability, and visualization of the fluorescent methods [5], different fluorescent dyes [7] and various fluorescent nanomaterials such as metal organic frameworks (MOFs) [8], quantum dots (QDs) [9], carbon dots (Cdots) [10], nanoclusters (NCs) [11], polymer dots (Pdots) [12], and silicon nanohybrids [13], have been introduced for the detection of Pb2+.

Since the accidental discovery of Cdots in 2004 [9], these new carbon-based nanomaterials have emerged as potent fluorescent nanoparticles for application in various fields of physics, chemistry, biology, medicine, and engineering. This is mainly owing to benefits such as chemical inertness, good biocompatibility, high photo‐and chemical stability, and ease of production [14,15]. Moreover, the physical and chemical properties of Cdots can be easily designed and tuned via surface, composition, and morphology modifications [16]. So far, numerous bottom-up and top-down methods have been used to synthesize Cdots, and different elements such as N, S, P, B, and Se have been utilized to modify their chemical and optical properties [14]. The large number of reports on utilizing Cdots for selective fluorescence sensing of different metal ions like Cu2+ [17], Hg2+ [18], Pb2+ [19], and Cr6+ [20], which only differ in doped elements and/or surface chemical groups, is a clear reason of this statement.

However, most reported fluorescent probes are single-intensity-based that suffer from a number of problems such as photobleaching of the probe, light scattering from the sample matrix, fluctuations of the excitation source, and variations in the probe concentration. From the technical point of view, most of these limitations can be overcome through ratiometric fluorescence sensing.

In the present work, we have reported an ideal ratiometric fluorescence probe for selective detection of Pb2+. The proposed probe shows two separate emission peaks at 522–692 nm that responded oppositely to Pb2+ and resulted in a clear visual fluorescence color change from green to yellow and red with the gradual addition of Pb2+. Briefly, we synthesized green-emitting Cdots by pyrolysis of m-aminophenol (m-AP), as a precursor source, in the presence of dimethylformamide and phosphoric acid. To design a Cdots-based probe for Pb2+ detection, the surface of the as-synthesized Cdots was modified with glutathione (GSH), which has a strong binding affinity for Pb2+ ion. Upon the addition of Pb2+, the chemical binding of GSH ligands at the surface of neighboring Cdots with Pb2+ ions leads to the aggregation of the GSH-modified Cdots. Interestingly, the aggregation of GSH-modified Cdots accomplished with arising a new well-separated emission peak at 692 nm. The proposed ratiometric fluorescent probe shows a high sensitivity to Pb2+ ion and presents high selectivity over other metal ions.

N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (NHS) and 1-ethyl-3-(3 dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), monopotassium-phosphate (KH2PO4), disodium-hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), dimethylformamide (DMF), o-aminophenol (o-AP), m-aminophenol (m-AP), p-aminophenol (p-AP), and glutathione (GSH) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Nitrate or chloride salts of metal ions were used for selectivity tests. All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) produced with a Milli-Q water purification system was used for all the experiments.

Fluorescence measurements were carried out on a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer equipped with a xenon flash lamp. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained with a PerkinElmer 16 PC FTIR spectrophotometer. The average size and polydispersity of Cdots were examined by the Philips CM-20 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and the laser light scattering technique over a Zetasizer (Nano ZS-ZEN 3600; Malvern Instruments).

The GSH-modified m-AP Cdots were prepared according to a two-step synthesis method. Briefly, m-AP (0.3 g) was dissolved in 10 mL DMF and 5 mL phosphoric acid solution, and the mixed solution was heated up to 200 °C for 15 min. After the reaction, the solidified brownish-yellow sample was cooled to room temperature, re-dispersed in 20 mL Milli-Q water, passed from a 0.22 µm cellulose acetate membrane syringe filter, and dried by a freeze dryer. The final Cdots solution with 2 mg mL−1 concentration was prepared for further use. To attach GSH molecules to the surface of Cdots, 2 mL of the fresh solution of EDC (2 mg mL−1) and NHS (1 mg mL−1) was added to 2 mL of the prepared Cdots, and the mixture was stirred for 4 h at room temperature. Subsequently, 10 mg GSH was introduced to the solution and further stirred for 6 h at room temperature. After terminating the reaction, the obtained solution was purified by a dialysis membrane (1500 Da, molecular weight cutoff) for 24 h to remove unreacted GSH molecules. The purified solution of GSH-modified Cdots (m-AP-GSH Cdots) was dried by a freeze dryer and its stock solution of 2 mg mL−1 was kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further analysis.

For the fluorescence measurements, 15 µL of stock solution of m-AP-GSH Cdots was diluted to 2 mL with 10 mM phosphate buffer (P.B.) at pH 7.0 to obtain a final concentration of 15 µg mL−1. Then, the above solution was titrated with varying concentrations of Pb2+ ions over the range of 10–1000 nM. In all cases, the fluorescence spectra were recorded after 2 min of Pb2+ addition from 460 to 750 nm, at a fixed excitation wavelength of 390 nm. For selectivity measurements, the required volumes of the stock solutions of different metal ions were added to 2 mL of m-AP-GSH Cdots (15 µg mL−1, 10 mM P.B. solution with pH 7.0), to have the final concentration of 1.0 µM of metal ions. The fluorescence spectrum of each solution was then recorded at room temperature after 2 min of mixing.

The modeled structures were optimized using the density functional theory (DFT) by the basis function Becke three-parameter hybrid density functional method (B3LYP) with 6–31 G(d) basis set for carbon, oxygen, hydrogen, nitrogen and sulfur atoms. The LANL2DZ basis set was applied to the Pb2+ ion. The absorption and emission calculations were performed by time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) with the CAM-B3LYP method. The maximum absorption wavelengths were calculated from the first electron excitation state (n = 1) at the S0 → S1 basis state, and the maximum emission wavelengths were obtained from the LUMO → HOMO electron transfer process of the first excitation state (S1 → S0) [21].

The two-step synthesis rout of m-AP-GSH Cdots is shown in Scheme 1. In the first step, Cdots were synthesized from the pyrolysis of m-AP in phosphoric acid and DMF. Then, the surfaces of m-AP Cdots were modified with GSH through the carbodiimide-activated coupling reaction between the glycyl carboxylic acid group of GSH and amino groups at the surfaces of m-AP Cdots.

TEM was employed to investigate the morphology and particle size distribution of m-AP-GSH Cdots ( Fig. 1a), where Cdots were appeared to be well-dispersed with an average size of 2.1 nm and a narrow size distribution. The XRD pattern in Fig. S1 illustrates a broad peak at about 2θ = 22◦, which can be allocated to a disordered structure of the synthesized Cdots [22]. Fig. 1b shows the FTIR spectra of GSH, m-AP Cdots, and m-AP-GSH Cdots. For m-AP Cdots, the broad peak at 3300–3500 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of the N–H and O–H groups [22], and peaks at 1220–1250 cm−1 and 1650–1550 cm−1 are owing to the stretching vibrations of C-O and C-N = groups, respectively. By attaching the GSH groups to the surface of m-AP Cdots, the stretching vibration peak of glycine carboxyl group of GSH at 1712 cm−1 is vanished and a peak related to the –SH group at about 2520 cm−1 is appeared. Since the unreacted GSH molecules were removed by the dialysis membrane, these results indicate the attachment of the GSH through the glycine carboxyl groups to the surface amino groups of m-AP Cdots.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of m-AP Cdots displayed two observable peaks at 240 and 328 nm (Fig. 1c). According to the literature, the absorption bands at 240 nm can be related to the π–π * transition of the aromatic CC bonds and n–π * transition of the CO bond, and the peak at 328 nm is related to the n–π * transition of the C-N bonds [23,24]. When GSH is conjugated to the surface, two new peaks at 370 and 450 nm appear. Since GSH individually does not show any absorption peak at wavelengths higher than 300 nm, the appearance of these new peaks indicates the positive effect of GSH in extending the conjugation structures of m-AP Cdots or creating new chromophore centers at the surface of Cdots.

The as-synthesized m-AP Cdots showed excitation-independent emission spectra with a maximum emission at 524 nm (Fig. S2) and a fluorescence quantum yield of 57.5%, using quinine sulfate as reference. The fluorescence properties of the Cdots showed negligible changes upon GSH conjugation, as the maximum emission wavelength just shifted to 522 nm (Fig. 1d) and their fluorescence quantum yield changed to 52.8%. The fluorescence stability of the m-AP-GSH Cdots in the presence of varying NaCl concentrations and pH values are also shown in Fig. S3. From the results, the fluorescence intensity showed a negligible variation even at NaCl concentrations up to 1.0 M, which shows the high resistant of the Cdots in high ionic strength solutions. The highest fluorescence intensity was observed at pH 7.0 and decreased with a slight slope by changing the pH to the acidic or basic values. Moreover, the m-AP-GSH Cdots exhibited an excellent photobleaching resistance under 365 nm UV irradiation (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of m-AP-GSH Cdots to the determination of Pb2+
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the application of unmodified m-AP Cdots as fluorescent probe in detection of metal ions, its fluorescence response in the presence of various metal ions including Li+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Ba2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Cr2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Cd2+, and Hg2+ was studied (Fig. S4). Interestingly, despite the existence of the amine groups on the surface of these Cdots, their fluorescence intensity showed no noticeable variation in the presence of the tested metal ions even at concentrations up to 1 µM. This result encouraged us to modify the surface of Cdots with a proper ligand for sensing purposes. GSH with two ending carboxyl, -NH, and -SH groups and a high affinity for binding to heavy metal ions [25], was selected as a potential candidate for this issue. In this regard, GSH molecules were covalently attached to the surface amino groups of m-AP Cdots. Upon this modification, a clear band at 2438 cm−1 was appeared in the FTIR spectrum of the m-AP-GSH Cdots that was attributed to the -SH stretching vibration [26] (see Fig. 1b). Since the synthesized m-AP-GSH Cdots were filtered through a 1500 dialysis bag to eliminate the unbounded GSH molecules, the appearance of this peak in the FTIR spectrum can be taken as evidence for the successful attachment of GSH to the surface of m-AP Cdots. Investigating the fluorescence response of the synthesized m-AP-GSH Cdots toward metal ions provided interesting results for Pb2+, in which the emission peak at 522 nm was selectively quenched and a new peak at 692 nm emerged. The distance between these emission peaks is 170 nm, which is much higher than the required wavelength difference (i.e., >80 nm) to be practically utilized in an ideal ratiometric fluorescent probe [33].

To get the highest response value of the probe (F692/F522) along the lowest limit of detection, the effective experimental factors including the probe solution pH, reaction time, and the probe concentration were first optimized (Fig. S5). Accordingly, the pH solution of 7.0 was found as the optimum value, and the fluorescence response obviously decreased at both basic and acidic pH values. This is probably due to the protonation of the surface functional groups of m-AP-GSH Cdots and formation of lead hydroxides, respectively. The response time study indicated a relatively fast response time of the probe with a stable value of 1 min, and the best fluorescence response was found for 15 µg mL−1 of m-AP-GSH Cdots when its concentration was monitored from 5 to 30 µg mL−1 in the presence of a constant concentration of Pb2+ (500 nM).

To evaluate the detection performance of the probe, the fluorescence spectra of m-AP-GSH Cdots in the presence of various concentrations of Pb2+ over the range of 10–1000 nM were recorded and the results are shown in Fig. 2a. As can be seen, the fluorescence intensity of the probe at 522 nm was gradually reduced while the intensity of the new emission band at 692 nm regularly increased with Pb2+ concentration. The ratiometric fluorescence response (F692 /F522) of the probe showed a satisfactory linear relationship with Pb2+ concentration, ranging from 10 to 700 nM, fitted to the equation of F692/F522 = 0.0015 [Pb2+] + 0.0318 (R2 = 0.9971), as shown in Fig. 2b. The limit of detection of this method was as low as 2.7 nM, as it was obtained based on a 3σ /k where σ is the standard deviation of the probe in the absence of Pb2+, and k is the slope of the linear equation. Fig. 2c shows the fluorescence spectra of the m-AP-GSH Cdots in the presence of different metal ions, which indicates the high selectivity of the probe toward Pb2+. The visual fluorescence response of m-AP-GSH Cdots to Pb2+ is also shown in the inset of Fig. 2c. The free probe exhibited an intense green emission that was changed to yellow and red upon addition of Pb2+ over a concentration range of 100–1000 nM. These data suggest that the m-AP-GSH Cdots can be used as a selective and sensitive probe for detection of Pb2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Pb2+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the designed ratiometric sensing probe was also assessed in environmental conditions. Three real water samples obtained from tap, river, and industrial waste waters of Zanjan as an industrial town of Iran, whose atmosphere is usually polluted with some metal particulates, including lead and zinc, and their Pb2+ contents were measured with the proposed method. The collected fresh samples were centrifuged at 14000 rpm for 15 min, filtered through a 0.22 µm cellulose acetate membrane syringe filter, and first analyzed by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES). No Pb2+ was detected in tap and river water samples but the concentration of Pb2+ in industrial waste water sample was found to be 2798 ppb (13.50 µM). To analyze the samples by the proposed probe, 2.0, 2.0, and 0.1 mL of these water samples, respectively, were transferred into a 3-mL cells and diluted to 2.5 mL by a P.B. solution (10 mM, pH 7.0) containing a final concentration of 15 µg mL−1 of m-AP-GSH Cdots. In good agreement with ICP-AES data, Pb2+ ions were not detected in the tab and river water samples, and Pb2+ concentration in the industrial sample after including the dilution factor was found to be 13.45 ± 0.07 µM (2787 ± 16 ppb) (n = 5). Moreover, different concentrations of 50, 200, and 600 nM Pb2+ were also spiked to the 100 times diluted industrial sample and analyzed with the probe. The results are shown in Table S1. The analyte recovery values based on the fluorescence measurement were found to be 98.0~103.1%. These results demonstrated that the proposed probe is largely free from the matrix effect of the river and industrial water samples and can reliably and efficiently detect Pb2+ in real water samples. Comparison of the analytical merits of the m-AP-GSH Cdots with several previously reported fluorescent nanoprobes for Pb2+ detection is listed in Table S2 that indicates the proposed method has been successful in measuring of lead ion.

As a starting point of the involved mechanism of the observed ratiometric florescence behavior, the average particle size of the m-AP-GSH Cdots in the presence and absence of Pb2+ ions was investigated. As depicted in Figs. 3a and 3b, the average dynamic size of the m-AP-GSH Cdots changed from 1.67 nm in the absence of Pb2+ to 289.3 nm after the addition of Pb2+, which indicates the strong aggregation of the Cdots with Pb2+. Moreover, the zeta potential of the Cdots changed from − 9.74 to + 0.42 mV. This is indicative of the fact that the surface negative charge of m-AP-GSH Cdots shifts to more positive values due to the binding of Pb2+ ions to the surface functional groups. This aggregation was also clearly observed in the corresponding TEM images, shown in Figs. 3a and 3b.

Therefore, it is evident that the presence of a strong metal ion chelating agent like ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) can dominate the formed complex and re-disperse the aggregated Cdots. To test this hypothesis, an equivalent amount of EDTA to Pb2+ was added to the aggregated m-AP-GSH Cdots solution. As can be seen from Fig. 3c, the quenched fluorescence by Pb2+ was completely restored upon addition of the same concentration of EDTA. Such Off-On cycle that was repeated each time upon the Pb2+/EDTA addition cycle, well verified that Pb2+ binds to the surface groups of the m-AP-GSH Cdots and no redox reaction is involved in the observed fluorescence variation. In fact, EDTA as a strong binding ligand prevents binding of Pb2+ to the surface functional groups of the probe via formation of a stronger Pb2+-EDTA complex (log Kf of 18.0 [27]) and the probe and its fluorescence value return to their states before the addition of Pb2+. It is also well-known that the aggregation of separated nanoparticles would increase their Rayleigh scattering intensity [28–30]. Accordingly, Fig. 3d shows the intensity of the Rayleigh scattering of m-AP-GSH Cdots solution that strongly increases with the addition of Pb2+ and returns to the initial value when an equimolar amount of EDTA is added to the solution [31].

A separate set of control studies were also carried out to investigate the possible response of Cdots synthesized by ortho and para isomers of AP to Pb2+, under the same experimental conditions. Both synthesized o-AP Cdots and p-AP Cdots presented a more or less excitation-independent emissions with maximum emission wavelengths around 500–520 nm (see Fig. S6). However, in contrast to m-AP-GSH Cdots, after reaction of both o- and p-AP Cdots with GSH, no evidence for –SH groups was found in their FTIR spectra, well-demonstrating the inability of GSH to bind to the surface amino groups of these Cdots (see Fig. S7). This observation can be related to the inaccessibility of their surface amino groups to the carboxyl group of GSH, due to the conjugation effect of the hydroxyl and amino groups at the ortho- and para-positions on benzo rings [32].

Moreover, no noticeable variation in the fluorescence intensity of the o-AP Cdots was observed in the presence of the tested metal ions, even up to 10 µM. Meanwhile, p-AP Cdots showed a gradual quenching upon addition of Fe3+ ions (Fig. S6). In this case, it can be speculated that the Fe3+ ions as oxidizing agents oxidize the surface functional groups of p-AP Cdots and quench their fluorescence. This hypothesis was confirmed after observing a similar quenching behavior with the gradual addition of potassium dichromate as a common oxidizing agent, as shown in Fig. S8. All these results demonstrated the critical role of the surface GSH groups in the fluorescence response of m-AP-GSH Cdots to Pb2+.

It is reported that the glycyl carboxylic acid and -NH, and -SH groups of GSH are available groups for probable binding with metal ions [33,34]. In fact, it has already been shown by Fuhr and Rabenstein [33] that the Pb2+ ion binds to the sulfhydryl group of GSH at pH 5.4–12.0. Here, the only other binding site of significance over this pH range is the glycyl carboxylic acid group that is involved in covalent binding with amino groups at the surface of m-AP Cdots. According to these reports, by addition of Pb2+ to GSH solution, a two-nuclear complex in which Pb2+ is bonded to the sulfhydryl groups of the two GSH ligands is formed, which matches well with disappearing the FTIR peak of –SH group on m-AP-GSH Cdots, upon addition of Pb2+ (Fig. 1b).

The theoretical calculations were also utilized to investigate the role of Pb2+ in the aggregation of m-AP-GSH Cdots and emerging the red emission peak. First of all, the optical properties of a series of nitrogen-doped aromatic-based structures containing an amino group attached to a GSH molecule were investigated using DFT and TD-DFT methods. Among these, a nine aromatic ring structure containing two nitrogen atoms and a surface hydroxyl group was selected as a modeled structure, as shown in Fig. 4a. This modeled structure presented the closest optical properties to the m-AP-GSH Cdots, with maximum absorption and emission peaks at 456 and 528 nm, respectively.

Electron and hole density distributions of the modeled structure was also investigated using the electron-hole theory to obtain more information about the charge transfer process in the discussed structures. To simplify the visual analysis of the results, the modeled structure was divided into 4 fragments with different electro-optical properties (see Fig. 5a). In this figure, fragment 1 concerns with the main part of the modeled structure for nitrogen-doped Cdots (i.e., the green atoms), fragment 2 assigns to the part of the modeled structure attached to the carboxyl group of GSH (i.e., the red atoms), fragment 3 contains the sulfur and amide parts of GSH (i.e., the yellow atoms) and finally, fragment 4 is the end of the attached GSH chain that is an amino acid functional group (i.e., the blue atoms).

The calculated results provided valuable information including the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) phenomenon in the six different excitation states of the modeled structure. Fig. 5a shows the heat map of the electron and hole orbital density distributions together with their overlap degrees on each fragments of the modeled structure for these excitation states in a relative and a visual way. The resulting distance and overlap between the hole and electron orbitals are also provided in Table S3. Moreover, Fig. 5 presents the same hole-electron transfers in the modeled structure by the molecular orbital (MO) theory, where the HOMO and LUMO orbitals participating with percentages above 15% in the final orbitals.

As can be seen from Fig. 5a, for these excitation states the excited electrons and holes are located on different fragments. In fact, when electrons of fragment 1 or 2 are exited, the electrons of fragment 3 (mostly sulfur lone pair) occupy HOMO of these fragment, and it is no longer possible for the excited electrons to return to the ground state directly. This process leads to an emission peak at a shorter wavelength. This assumption was confirmed when it was found that replacing the sulfur atom in the GSH of the modeled structure with a carbon atom changed the maximum emission peak to 622 nm, so that there was no PET process and the electrons and holes were recombined directly (see Fig. S9).

Interestingly, Pb2+ ion forms a complex with sulfur atoms of GSH groups and somehow near oxygen atoms, when it is placed between the modeled structures. This complexation resulted in shifting the maximum absorption peak to 504 nm and even a much more noticeable shift in the fluorescence emission spectrum, as the maximum emission peak shifted from 528 nm to 648 nm, an increase of 120 nm (see Fig. 4b). It is interesting to note that, after the addition of Pb2+, the sulfur lone pair electrons of the surface GSH group participate in the complex formation with the Pb2+ and, therefore, the PET process is stopped. As can be seen from Fig. 5b and Table S3, a significant increase occurred in the overlap degree of electron and hole density distributions for the same excitation state of the modeled structure in the complexed form, which is due to the locating of the excited electrons and holes on the same fragment of the modeled structure. The calculated hole and electron orbitals for the modeled structure of m-AP-GSH Cdots in the six assigned excitation wavelengths and participating HOMO and LUMO orbitals are also shown in Fig. 6. These results clearly confirm the observed experimental red-shifts in both the absorption and the emission spectra of m-AP-GSH Cdots after addition of Pb2+ ion.

Heavy metal ions are known to be among the most hazardous pollutants, causing serious damage to both the environment and human health. Thus, developing simple, rapid, and reliable detection methods for these toxic ions is crucial. Cdots are a new class of fluorescent probes that can be produced simply and inexpensively, which respond rapidly to the presence of an analyte. In this study, we provided a highly sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescent probe for the determination of Pb2+ based on a simple surface modification of Cdots. The provided probe displayed two separate emission peaks that were excited with a single excitation wavelength and responded oppositely to Pb2+ showing a clear color variation under the addition of Pb2+ at nano molar concentration levels. The theoretical calculations demonstrated how the optical properties of Cdots can be changed after surface modification, and how the surface modification can lead to new emission states and electron transfer processes in the modified Cdots. This report emphasizes that the surface modification applied to the Cdots can be considered as one of the most important methods to regulate their optical and sensing behaviors.

Conceptualization, Mojtaba Shamsipur, Methodology / Study design, Ali Barati, Foroozan Feizi, Software, Avat (Arman) Taherpour, Morteza Jamshidi, Formal analysis, Sardar Paydar, Neda Chehri, Investigation, Sardar Paydar, Foroozan Feizi, Data curation, Ali barati, Writing – original draft, Sardar Paydar, Foroozan Feizi, Writing – review and editing, Mojtaba Shamsipur, Ali barati, Visualization, Ali barati, Supervision, Ali barati, Project administration, Mojtaba Shamsipur.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the support of Iran Nanotechnology Initiative Council (INIC).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132243.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Sardar Paydar is a PhD student in Analytical chemistry at Razi university, Kermanshah, Iran under supervision Prof. Shamsipur and Dr Barati. His interests include synthesis and application of fluorescent nanoparticles, and their modification of by biomolecules for development of optical nanoprobes.

Foroozan Feizi has received her PhD in Analytical Chemistry from Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran in 2021 under the supervision of Prof. Mojtaba Shamsipur. She is currently a postdoctoral member of Shamsipur’s research group to study about synthesis and characterization of florescent polymer dots and their applications in develop new fluorescenct bio-sensing and bio-imaging systems.

Mojtaba Shamsipur has a PhD in analytical chemistry obtained at Michigan State University in 1979. He is currently a professor of chemistry at Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran. His research work has been mainly focused on the thermodynamic and kinetic studies of macrocyclic ligand complexes, and their analytical applications in areas such as ion-transport through liquid membranes, solid-phase extraction, ion-selective electrodes, and optical sensors. From the last decade, his research area is also extended to fluorescent nanoparticles and their analytical applications. He has published over 1000 scientific papers in international journals.

Ali Barati is currently an assistant professor of analytical chemistry at Razi University. His interest include synthesis and application of fluorescent nanoparticles and utilizing chemometrics methods in analytical applications of these nanoparticles.

Neda Chehri is a PhD student in Analytical chemistry at Razi university, Kermanshah, Iran under supervision Prof. Mojtaba Shamsipur. His interests include synthesis and application of fluorescent nanoparticles specially carbon dots and their phosphorescence properties.

Avat (Arman) Taherpour is currently a professor of organic chemistry at Razi University. His main research interests are focused on organic synthesis, nano chemistry, computational chemistry, molecular modeling and theoretical chemistry. He has published over 150 articles in international journals.
Morteza Jamshidi is a graduate student at Razi University working toward his master degree under the supervision of Prof. Taherpour.
